title: Cat Green Thick Nasal Discharge: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what thick green nasal discharge in cats means, why it requires a vet visit, and how photos help with triage. Urgency Level: Medium.
content_html:
What is Green Nasal Discharge?

Thick, green discharge from your cat's nose is technically known as mucopurulent discharge. Unlike clear fluid, which can be caused by simple irritation or allergies, green or yellow mucus typically indicates a secondary bacterial infection. This happens when the immune system sends white blood cells to the nasal passages to fight off an underlying issue, such as a viral infection (like Feline Calicivirus or Rhinotracheitis), a fungal infection, or even a foreign object stuck in the nose.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While a cat with green nasal discharge is rarely in immediate, life-threatening danger, they are likely feeling quite unwell and require veterinary attention within 24 to 48 hours. Cats rely heavily on their sense of smell to stimulate their appetite; if their nose is blocked, they may stop eating, which can lead to further complications like dehydration or liver issues. You should upgrade this to an emergency if your cat is gasping for air, breathing with an open mouth, or has completely stopped eating.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of your cat’s face and the discharge itself is an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. Because discharge can sometimes be sneezed away or cleaned off by the cat before you reach the clinic, a photo provides proof of the color, thickness, and volume. It also allows the vet to see if the discharge is coming from one nostril or both, which helps differentiate between a localized issue—like a nasal polyp—and a systemic respiratory infection.

faqs.question: Can I use human nasal sprays on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications on cats without a vet's direct instruction. Many human decongestants are toxic to felines.

faqs.question: Is green nasal discharge contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the cause is viral or bacterial, it can be highly contagious to other cats in the household. It is best to isolate the sick cat until you see a vet.

faqs.question: Will this clear up on its own?
faqs.answer: Green discharge usually indicates a bacterial component that requires prescription antibiotics. It is unlikely to resolve fully without medical intervention.
